Triggers.value: Being in a natural environment similar to where it happened

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I dwell on what other people have done to me
keep mind occupied all the time
try hard not think about the trauma

Threat.value: I study people's faces and body language to detect if they are a threat
I have difficulty falling asleep because I need to stay alert
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: Sometimes I can recall what happened but feel completely numb, as if it happened to a stranger
When I remember the event I feel intense shame
My memories of the frightening event are mostly pictures or images.

issues.explanation: This trigger is hard to reconcile with the rest of the profile. For terrorism-related PTSD, environmental/somatic reminders are plausible, but object throwing is an odd, highly specific trigger that is not supported by the memory or threat items and does not fit the described formulation as well as the other triggers.

issues.item: Avoid Similar Clothes: avoid wearing similar clothes
issues.explanation: This avoidance is not clearly coherent with the trauma context or with the other selected strategies. Clothing-specific avoidance is possible, but here it appears disconnected from the terrorism-related triggers and memory content, making it clinically harder to integrate with the rest of the profile.

issues.item: Safe Zone Only: I only feel safe in a very limited number of places
issues.explanation: This implies broad ongoing generalized threat and hypervigilance, but the formulation has no active Triggers→Threat or Threat→{Memory/Triggers/Appraisals} edges. The rest of the profile is more cue/memory-linked than pervasive threat-based, so this item pulls in a direction that is not well supported.

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This conflicts with the other memory items, especially intense shame on recall and emotional numbing on recall. Easy verbal access can coexist with PTSD, but paired with the selected items it suggests a different memory presentation than the rest of the node.

vignette:
Marko is a 43-year-old European roofer who remains distressed by a terrorist attack he witnessed and narrowly escaped. He had been working outdoors in a wooded, rural area when the attack unfolded, and the moment that still stays sharp for him is the sense of immediate danger while he was exposed in the open, with the sound and smell of the scene pressing in around him. Since then, natural settings similar to that landscape, the sensation of his own heart pounding, and the smell of alcohol can all bring the episode back into the foreground. At work, when he is up on a roof and his pulse starts to race, he becomes unsettled and preoccupied. He also notices that a whiff of alcohol on someone’s breath can hit him with the same jolt.

When Marko talks about the event, he often describes it in images rather than a continuous story. He will stop mid-sentence and say he can see only fragments: a stretch of ground, a sudden movement, a face, a body position. At other times he says he can remember what happened but it feels oddly distant, “like it happened to somebody else,” and he sounds flat while saying it. Yet when the memory returns, he also becomes visibly ashamed, lowering his eyes and speaking as if he cannot bear what he is recalling.

Marko’s central stuck point is that the attack has left him feeling deeply ashamed and mistrustful: in his own words, “something about me should have been different, and people are not safe.” He speaks as if the event exposed him to a world in which other people can suddenly turn dangerous, and in which he cannot fully trust his own judgment.

He studies people’s faces and body language for any sign of danger, especially when he is around unfamiliar men or when he is on a job site and has to rely on others nearby. At night he has trouble falling asleep because he feels he needs to stay alert. He also reports sudden moments when he feels and acts as if the trauma is happening again.

The most prominent pattern is that reminders of the event pull him into the memory, and the memory then pushes him back toward more reminders and more coping efforts. A racing heart, the smell of alcohol, or being in a similar natural setting can bring the images on; once they do, he becomes more likely to try hard not to think about it, to keep his mind occupied all the time, and to dwell on what other people have done to him. This cycle has begun to affect his marriage and his work, because he is mentally elsewhere during conversations at home and remains tense and watchful while roofing, even when nothing obvious is wrong.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The sentence states that triggers ('A racing heart, the smell of alcohol, or being in a similar natural setting') 'can bring the images on; once they do, he becomes more likely to try hard not to think about it, to keep his mind occupied all the time...'. This gives explicit causal flow from triggers to coping/maladaptive strategies within the same sentence.
satisfied_edges.quote: A racing heart, the smell of alcohol, or being in a similar natural setting can bring the images on; once they do, he becomes more likely to try hard not to think about it, to keep his mind occupied all the time, and to dwell on what other people have done to him.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The same sentence says triggers 'can bring the images on,' which is explicit causal language from triggers to memory/images, satisfying the required direction.
satisfied_edges.quote: A racing heart, the smell of alcohol, or being in a similar natural setting can bring the images on; once they do, he becomes more likely to try hard not to think about it, to keep his mind occupied all the time, and to dwell on what other people have done to him.

satisfied_edges.explanation: The clause 'once they do, he becomes more likely to try hard not to think about it, to keep his mind occupied all the time...' shows the memory/images leading to maladaptive coping strategies in the same sentence.
